item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the 1998 crisis happened because something that was nailed to the wall fell down after years of disuse.

My name is Gaffney, and I approve this headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not by 1990, I'm afraid. I wonder if this means the Union's collapse was in some way caused by the GRU operatives fiddling with things to see what they affected.

Headcanon: the 1998 crisis happened because something that was nailed to the wall fell down after years of disuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not noted by the observers: considering that one of the main problems with the Soviet Union was the inability of leaders to directly observe economic conditions or to calculate output thresholds effectively, this room would have been absolutely Thaumiel to GRU-P.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! The issues with apartment size and okrug letter-having are all fixed. As for Podlogistan, I think it originally appears in SLATE THUNDER; I mostly just included it as a good and/or excuse to include more Central Asian countries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2214 is a 85 m2 apartment located in ██████ Okrug, Moscow. The layout of SCP-2214 is consistent with late Soviet-era standards for single-resident apartments

First off, there's no six-letter Okrug in Moscow. I suggest you replace it with [REDACTED] so that it leaves no place for guesswork.
Second, 85 square meters for a single resident? That's rich. No, that's RICH. Most of my life was spent in a ~55 m2 apartment housing a family of five. 85 m2 is a rather spacey three- or four-room apartment. If you trim it down to 35 meters, that would seem believable.

UPD: I found other mentions of Podlogistan on the Hornby archive, but it doesn't seem to be a good name. "Подлог" ("Podlog") stands for "fraud" or "forgery", Russian readers would likely be amused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is wierd and I like it, but I felt like the object list could have been longer.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, are the objects in the apartment only an instrument panel that (weirdly) can be used to observe changes in the relevant economic conditions, or can they be used in the opposite direction, to brute-force changes in those economic conditions? In other words, could you (for example) change the Sakha Republic's gross annual exports of tin by intentionally altering the position of a teacup in the apartment? And if so, did this anomaly contribute to the cause of the fall of the USSR?

I'm dumb and didn't read the article closely enough. The article answers these questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally 2214 has a successful SCP, I wonder when will 2186 find his.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is both a very simple, and a very fun article. The concept is neat, and I found the writing to be humorous in more than a few places.

+1. Its pretty neatO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know. 2188 and this are different in terms of scope of story. It's hard to, in an SCP article, expand on the cumulative effects changing an object in 2214 can do without some serious and thorough exposition that would assuredly not be entertaining to read. It would also probably be beyond the scope of many writers. By keeping it simple and constrained like this, one is able to fill in the gaps in their head and apply the object to their headcanon as needed. 2188 works more on a human scale, which is how it can get away with dumping information and narrative. This is fine as it is, and it's a good example of a short article with story and interesting detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weird, but there's really nothing else to it. That can work sometimes if the concept is weird or unique enough, but here it doesn't do it for me. It reads like 2188 if 2188 was just the anomaly, with no further information or narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Synecdoche, Soviet Russia.

I like this.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Followed by this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of me wants to headcanon Class-R amnestic as a bottle of really good Vodka and a conversation about how silly it would be to talk about what you just saw while drinking it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, the entire article is well written, but that's just hilarious, had I been neutral, that would have been my upvote1.

Footnotes
1. Meaning that I would have upvoted at that line, had I not done so already

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure what a Class-R amnestic is, but I like this. It's kind of like that one about the town in Maine (?) and the guy from Argentina (???). That many objects affecting such disparate things, not to mention the fact that new items can be added. Wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a simplicity of an old style SCP to it, in a good way. It's also weird and interesting, and shows familiarity with (former) Soviet Union living standards. Solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very cool and very unique. I gave an enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think 2214 finally found the SCP that belongs to it. And a very fine one it is.

Edit: also, "killed when a dresser, later discovered to be affected by the state-mandated price for Uzbek cotton, fell on top of him" may be one of the strangest ways to die ever concieved.

item.title: Alternate Title: A Communal Apartment as the USSR
item.content:encoded:
Wow, it's been a really long time since I last posted a SCP article. Decided to keep it short this time.

Many thanks to vezaz, VAE, rumetzen, and coony for taking the time to critique.
